question: In the recommended data-accessor pattern, what is the purpose of a Process Variable Accessor?

options.text: It encapsulates the access to exactly one variable and is useful when the same variable is reused across different process definitions.
It knows the names and types of all process variables of one process definition and is the central point to declare them.
It stores complex business data in a relational database alongside the process so the engine only holds a reference.
It is a Java interface of `String` constants holding the variable names so the IDE can refactor them safely.

a.text: The docs describe a Process Variable Accessor as a class that encapsulates access to exactly one variable, which makes it the right choice when the same variable is reused across different processes.

b.text: That description matches a Process Data Accessor, which knows the names and types of all process variables of a certain process definition and serves as the central declaration point.

c.text: Storing complex data in a relational database is the separate 'complex data as entities' pattern, not what either data accessor flavor does.

d.text: An interface of `String` constants is the simpler 'constants' pattern the docs introduce first; data accessors go further by handling type conversion and serialization in addition to naming.

explanation: The handling-data-in-processes best practice identifies two flavors of Data Accessor: a Process Data Accessor (knows the names and types of all variables of one process definition) and a Process Variable Accessor (encapsulates access to exactly one variable, useful when that variable is reused in different processes). Plain `String` constants are a simpler related pattern that solves only the naming/typo problem.
